nodes.name: Linear Algebra
nodes.description: The study of vectors, matrices, linear transformations, and vector spaces. It provides the language used to represent datasets, model parameters, embeddings, and neural network layers.
nodes.importance: Most machine learning systems store data and model operations as vectors and matrices, so linear algebra is a core foundation for understanding how models compute.

nodes.name: Probability Theory
nodes.description: A mathematical framework for reasoning about uncertainty, random variables, distributions, expectation, and conditional dependence.
nodes.importance: Machine learning uses probability to model uncertain data, evaluate predictions, estimate risk, and reason about noisy observations.

nodes.name: Calculus
nodes.description: The study of change through derivatives, gradients, limits, and integrals. In machine learning it is most often used to understand how a loss changes as model parameters change.
nodes.importance: Gradients make it possible to improve model parameters systematically instead of searching blindly.

nodes.name: Statistics
nodes.description: Methods for summarizing data, estimating unknown quantities, testing hypotheses, and measuring uncertainty from observations.
nodes.importance: Statistics gives machine learning its language for generalization, sampling, estimation, bias, variance, and evaluation.

nodes.name: Optimization
nodes.description: The study of choosing values that minimize or maximize an objective function under constraints.
nodes.importance: Training a machine learning model is usually an optimization problem: find parameters that minimize prediction error while controlling complexity.

nodes.name: Gradient Descent
nodes.description: An iterative optimization method that updates parameters in the direction that most reduces a loss function locally.
nodes.importance: Gradient descent and its variants are central to training linear models, neural networks, and many large-scale machine learning systems.

references.title: An overview of gradient descent optimization algorithms

nodes.name: Supervised Learning
nodes.description: A learning setting where models are trained on input examples paired with known target labels or values.
nodes.importance: Supervised learning powers many practical prediction systems, including classification, regression, ranking, and forecasting.

nodes.name: Decision Trees
nodes.description: A supervised learning model that recursively splits data by feature conditions to make predictions through a tree of decisions.
nodes.importance: Decision trees are intuitive, interpretable, and foundational for stronger ensemble methods such as random forests and gradient boosted trees.

nodes.name: Neural Networks
nodes.description: Parameterized models built from layers of simple differentiable functions that can learn complex mappings from data.
nodes.importance: Neural networks are the foundation of modern deep learning systems for vision, language, speech, robotics, and scientific modeling.

nodes.name: Backpropagation
nodes.description: An efficient algorithm for computing gradients through layered differentiable models by applying the chain rule backward from the loss.
nodes.importance: Backpropagation made practical training of multi-layer neural networks possible and remains a core mechanism in deep learning frameworks.

nodes.name: Discrete Mathematics
nodes.description: The study of countable structures such as sets, relations, logic, combinatorics, and proof techniques used to reason about finite systems.
nodes.importance: Discrete mathematics underpins algorithms, data structures, graphs, programming languages, cryptography, and many models of computation.

references.title: MIT OpenCourseWare Mathematics for Computer Science

nodes.name: Graph Theory
nodes.description: A branch of mathematics that models relationships with vertices and edges, including trees, paths, cycles, networks, and connectivity.
nodes.importance: Graphs provide a compact language for representing dependencies, search spaces, networks, computation flows, and many knowledge structures.

nodes.name: Algorithms
nodes.description: Finite, precise procedures for solving computational problems, transforming inputs into outputs, or making decisions step by step.
nodes.importance: Algorithms turn mathematical ideas into repeatable methods and make it possible to compare solutions by correctness, efficiency, and scalability.

nodes.name: Data Structures
nodes.description: Ways of organizing data in memory or storage so that operations such as lookup, insertion, deletion, and traversal can be performed efficiently.
nodes.importance: Data structures make algorithms practical by matching the representation of information to the operations a system needs to perform.

nodes.name: Programming Languages
nodes.description: Formal languages and execution models used to express algorithms, manage abstraction, control state, and build software systems.
nodes.importance: Programming languages make algorithms executable and shape how people design, verify, maintain, and scale technological systems.

nodes.name: Computer Architecture
nodes.description: The design of computer processors, memory systems, instruction sets, and input-output structures that determine how software runs on hardware.
nodes.importance: Architecture choices constrain speed, energy use, memory access, parallelism, and the feasibility of large-scale computation.

references.title: Computer Organization and Design RISC-V Edition

nodes.name: Parallel Computing
nodes.description: The practice of dividing computation across multiple processing units so work can proceed concurrently.
nodes.importance: Parallel computing makes large simulations, data processing pipelines, scientific workloads, and machine learning training feasible at useful scales.

nodes.name: GPU Computing
nodes.description: The use of graphics processing units for general-purpose, massively parallel numerical computation.
nodes.importance: GPU computing accelerates matrix operations and tensor workloads, making modern deep learning and many scientific simulations practical.

nodes.name: Information Theory
nodes.description: A mathematical theory of information, entropy, compression, coding, and communication over noisy channels.
nodes.importance: Information theory provides tools for reasoning about uncertainty, representation, compression, prediction, and limits on communication.

nodes.name: Numerical Methods
nodes.description: Algorithms for approximating mathematical operations such as solving equations, optimizing functions, integrating systems, and computing with finite precision.
nodes.importance: Numerical methods make continuous mathematics executable on real computers, where exact symbolic answers are often unavailable or impractical.

nodes.name: Automatic Differentiation
nodes.description: A family of techniques for computing derivatives of programs by propagating derivative information through elementary operations.
nodes.importance: Automatic differentiation powers modern machine learning frameworks by making gradient computation reliable, efficient, and composable.

references.title: Automatic Differentiation in Machine Learning: A Survey

nodes.name: Linear Regression
nodes.description: A supervised modeling method that estimates a linear relationship between input variables and a numeric target.
nodes.importance: Linear regression is a fundamental baseline for prediction, statistical inference, model evaluation, and understanding more complex supervised methods.

nodes.name: Bayesian Inference
nodes.description: A framework for updating beliefs about unknown quantities by combining prior assumptions with observed evidence through probability.
nodes.importance: Bayesian inference gives data analysis and machine learning a principled way to express uncertainty, compare models, and incorporate prior knowledge.

nodes.name: Unsupervised Learning
nodes.description: A learning setting where algorithms search for structure, patterns, clusters, or compressed representations in data without explicit target labels.
nodes.importance: Unsupervised learning helps explore datasets, reduce dimensionality, discover groups, and build representations when labeled data is scarce.

nodes.name: Reinforcement Learning
nodes.description: A learning framework where agents choose actions through interaction with an environment and improve behavior using rewards or costs.
nodes.importance: Reinforcement learning connects machine learning to control, planning, robotics, games, and sequential decision-making under uncertainty.

nodes.name: Ensemble Methods
nodes.description: Methods that combine multiple models so their collective predictions are more accurate, stable, or robust than a single model alone.
nodes.importance: Ensembles such as random forests and boosting are practical workhorses for tabular data and demonstrate how weak models can form strong systems.

nodes.name: Representation Learning
nodes.description: Approaches that learn useful features or internal encodings from data instead of relying entirely on manually designed representations.
nodes.importance: Representation learning reduces the need for hand-crafted features and is a major reason neural networks can adapt across vision, language, and science tasks.

references.title: Representation Learning: A Review and New Perspectives

nodes.name: Deep Learning
nodes.description: A family of machine learning methods based on neural networks with many layers that learn hierarchical representations from data.
nodes.importance: Deep learning transformed computer vision, speech recognition, natural language processing, robotics, and scientific modeling through scalable representation learning.

nodes.name: Transformers
nodes.description: Neural network architectures built around attention mechanisms that model relationships among tokens or other sequence elements.
nodes.importance: Transformers became the dominant architecture for large language models and many multimodal systems because they scale well with data and compute.

nodes.name: Large Language Models
nodes.description: Large neural language models trained on massive text or multimodal corpora to predict, generate, transform, and reason over sequences.
nodes.importance: Large language models are a major modern technology platform for search, coding, writing, education, scientific assistance, and human-computer interaction.

nodes.name: Datasets
nodes.description: Collections of examples used to train, validate, test, benchmark, or analyze machine learning systems.
nodes.importance: Dataset design strongly shapes what a model can learn, which populations or conditions it represents, and how trustworthy evaluation results are.

nodes.name: Labels
nodes.description: Target values, classes, annotations, or judgments attached to examples so a supervised model has something to predict.
nodes.importance: Label quality often sets an upper bound on supervised learning performance because noisy or biased labels teach the wrong target behavior.

nodes.name: Features
nodes.description: Measurable input variables or learned representations used by a model to make predictions or discover structure.
nodes.importance: Feature choices determine what information is available to a model and can dominate performance in classical machine learning systems.

nodes.name: Train/Validation/Test Split
nodes.description: A data partitioning practice that separates examples used for fitting, model selection, and final evaluation.
nodes.importance: Clean splits help estimate generalization and reduce the chance that model choices are tuned to the final test set.

nodes.name: Data Preprocessing
nodes.description: The preparation of raw data into a representation suitable for modeling, including cleaning, scaling, encoding, and transformation.
nodes.importance: Preprocessing turns messy observations into model-ready inputs and often determines whether algorithms behave reliably.

nodes.name: Feature Scaling
nodes.description: Transforming numeric features onto comparable ranges or distributions, such as standardization or min-max scaling.
nodes.importance: Many distance-based and gradient-based methods behave poorly when features have very different scales.

nodes.name: Categorical Encoding
nodes.description: Representing categories as numeric values, indicator columns, target encodings, or learned embeddings so algorithms can process them.
nodes.importance: Encoding decisions affect model capacity, leakage risk, memory use, and how categorical structure is exposed to learning algorithms.

nodes.name: Missing Data Imputation
nodes.description: Methods for replacing missing values with estimates, indicators, or model-based predictions before or during learning.
nodes.importance: Real datasets often have missing values, and the handling strategy can change both model performance and interpretation.

nodes.name: Data Leakage
nodes.description: A modeling error where training or validation uses information that would not be available at prediction time.
nodes.importance: Leakage can create deceptively strong evaluation results while producing models that fail in real deployment.

nodes.name: Feature Engineering
nodes.description: Designing, transforming, combining, or selecting input variables so models can more easily capture useful patterns.
nodes.importance: Feature engineering remains a major driver of model quality, especially for tabular, scientific, and production systems with structured data.

nodes.name: Empirical Risk Minimization
nodes.description: A learning principle that chooses a model by minimizing average loss on observed training examples.
nodes.importance: Empirical risk minimization gives supervised learning a formal bridge between data, loss functions, optimization, and generalization.

references.title: The Nature of Statistical Learning Theory

nodes.name: Loss Functions
nodes.description: Functions that measure how costly a model prediction is relative to a target, preference, or desired behavior.
nodes.importance: The loss function defines what training optimizes and can change which errors a model learns to avoid.

nodes.name: Generalization
nodes.description: A model's ability to perform well on unseen examples drawn from the task distribution rather than only memorizing training data.
nodes.importance: Generalization is the central goal of machine learning and the reason careful evaluation matters.

nodes.name: Overfitting
nodes.description: A failure mode where a model fits training data too closely and performs poorly on new data.
nodes.importance: Recognizing overfitting helps practitioners choose simpler models, more data, regularization, or better validation strategies.

nodes.name: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
nodes.description: A framework that decomposes prediction error into systematic error, sensitivity to training data, and irreducible noise.
nodes.importance: The tradeoff explains why both overly simple and overly flexible models can generalize poorly.

nodes.name: Regularization
nodes.description: Techniques that constrain or penalize models to improve generalization, reduce variance, or encode preferences about solutions.
nodes.importance: Regularization is one of the main practical tools for controlling overfitting across linear models, kernels, and neural networks.

nodes.name: Cross-Validation
nodes.description: A model evaluation strategy that repeatedly trains and tests on different data splits to estimate performance more robustly.
nodes.importance: Cross-validation helps compare models and tune choices when a single validation split would be noisy or wasteful.

nodes.name: Hyperparameter Tuning
nodes.description: Choosing model or training settings that are not directly learned from data, such as depth, penalty strength, or learning rate.
nodes.importance: Hyperparameters can strongly affect accuracy, speed, memory use, and robustness, so tuning is a core workflow step.

nodes.name: Classification Metrics
nodes.description: Measures such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and related summaries for evaluating categorical predictions.
nodes.importance: Different classification metrics emphasize different error types, which matters when classes are imbalanced or error costs differ.

nodes.name: Regression Metrics
nodes.description: Measures such as mean squared error, mean absolute error, and R-squared for evaluating numeric predictions.
nodes.importance: Regression metrics make model errors comparable and express whether large mistakes, average mistakes, or explained variation matter most.

nodes.name: Calibration
nodes.description: The agreement between predicted probabilities and observed frequencies, often assessed or improved after classification.
nodes.importance: Well-calibrated probabilities are important for decision systems where confidence estimates affect actions, risk, or triage.

nodes.name: Confusion Matrix
nodes.description: A table that counts predicted versus true classes, exposing false positives, false negatives, and class-specific errors.
nodes.importance: Confusion matrices make classification failures concrete and support more targeted metric selection and error analysis.

nodes.name: Logistic Regression
nodes.description: A linear classification model that estimates class probabilities through a logistic or softmax link function.
nodes.importance: Logistic regression is a strong baseline for classification and a useful bridge between statistics, linear models, and probabilistic prediction.

nodes.name: Naive Bayes
nodes.description: A family of probabilistic classifiers that apply Bayes' rule with a simplifying conditional independence assumption among features.
nodes.importance: Naive Bayes is fast, interpretable, and historically important for text classification and other high-dimensional problems.

nodes.name: k-Nearest Neighbors
nodes.description: A non-parametric method that predicts from the labels or values of nearby training examples in a chosen feature space.
nodes.importance: k-nearest neighbors shows how representation, distance metrics, and local similarity can define learning without explicit training parameters.

nodes.name: Support Vector Machines
nodes.description: Supervised models that find separating margins for classification or regression, often using kernels for nonlinear boundaries.
nodes.importance: Support vector machines connect convex optimization, geometry, regularization, and kernel methods in a durable classical ML framework.

nodes.name: Kernel Methods
nodes.description: Methods that compute similarities through kernel functions, allowing linear algorithms to act in implicit feature spaces.
nodes.importance: Kernel methods made nonlinear learning mathematically tractable and influenced support vector machines, Gaussian processes, and similarity-based modeling.

nodes.name: Random Forests
nodes.description: Ensembles of decision trees trained with randomness in examples and features to reduce variance and improve prediction stability.
nodes.importance: Random forests are robust, practical baselines for tabular data and demonstrate the power of averaging many noisy models.

nodes.name: Gradient Boosting
nodes.description: An ensemble technique that builds models sequentially so each new model improves the current prediction by following a loss gradient.
nodes.importance: Gradient boosting is one of the most effective families of methods for structured and tabular prediction tasks.

references.title: Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine

nodes.name: Gaussian Processes
nodes.description: Bayesian non-parametric models that define distributions over functions using covariance kernels.
nodes.importance: Gaussian processes provide calibrated uncertainty and flexible function modeling, especially valuable for small-data regression and Bayesian optimization.

nodes.name: Semi-Supervised Learning
nodes.description: A learning setting that uses a small amount of labeled data together with larger amounts of unlabeled data.
nodes.importance: Semi-supervised learning is useful when labels are expensive but unlabeled data is plentiful.

nodes.name: Clustering
nodes.description: Unsupervised methods that group examples according to similarity, density, connectivity, or probabilistic structure.
nodes.importance: Clustering supports exploratory analysis, segmentation, compression, and discovery when explicit labels are unavailable.

nodes.name: k-Means
nodes.description: A clustering algorithm that partitions data into k groups by repeatedly assigning points to centroids and updating those centroids.
nodes.importance: k-means is a simple, scalable baseline for cluster discovery and vector quantization.

nodes.name: Hierarchical Clustering
nodes.description: Clustering methods that build nested group structures by repeatedly merging or splitting clusters.
nodes.importance: Hierarchical clustering helps reveal multi-scale structure and can be inspected without choosing a single flat partition first.

nodes.name: DBSCAN
nodes.description: A density-based clustering method that groups points in dense regions and marks isolated points as noise.
nodes.importance: DBSCAN can find irregularly shaped clusters and identify outliers without specifying the number of clusters in advance.

references.title: A Density-Based Algorithm for Discovering Clusters in Large Spatial Databases with Noise

nodes.name: Dimensionality Reduction
nodes.description: Methods that represent high-dimensional data with fewer variables while preserving selected structure or variation.
nodes.importance: Dimensionality reduction supports visualization, compression, denoising, and faster modeling on complex datasets.

nodes.name: Principal Component Analysis
nodes.description: A linear dimensionality reduction method that finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance in data.
nodes.importance: PCA is a foundational method for compression, visualization, denoising, and understanding linear structure in datasets.

nodes.name: Manifold Learning
nodes.description: A family of methods that assume high-dimensional data lies near lower-dimensional curved structures.
nodes.importance: Manifold learning motivates nonlinear visualization and representation methods for data with complex geometry.

nodes.name: t-SNE
nodes.description: A nonlinear embedding method that visualizes high-dimensional data by preserving local neighbor relationships.
nodes.importance: t-SNE became a widely used exploratory visualization tool for embeddings, images, biological data, and learned representations.

nodes.name: Gaussian Mixture Models
nodes.description: Probabilistic models that represent data as a mixture of Gaussian distributions with latent component assignments.
nodes.importance: Gaussian mixture models provide soft clustering and illustrate how latent-variable models can capture multimodal data.

nodes.name: Expectation-Maximization
nodes.description: An iterative algorithm for maximum likelihood estimation in latent-variable models by alternating expectation and maximization steps.
nodes.importance: Expectation-maximization is a central method for fitting models with hidden structure, including mixture models.

references.title: Maximum Likelihood from Incomplete Data via the EM Algorithm

nodes.name: Matrix Factorization
nodes.description: Methods that approximate a matrix as a product of lower-rank factors, often revealing latent structure.
nodes.importance: Matrix factorization supports recommender systems, dimensionality reduction, topic models, and representation learning.

references.title: Matrix Factorization Techniques for Recommender Systems

nodes.name: Tensors
nodes.description: Multi-dimensional arrays that generalize scalars, vectors, and matrices for numerical computation.
nodes.importance: Tensors are the basic data structure for modern deep learning frameworks, representing batches, activations, parameters, images, text, and gradients.

nodes.name: Computational Graphs
nodes.description: Graph representations of computations where nodes are operations or values and edges show data dependencies.
nodes.importance: Computational graphs make automatic differentiation, optimization, scheduling, and deployment of neural models tractable.

references.title: Automatic Differentiation in Machine Learning: A Survey

nodes.name: Activation Functions
nodes.description: Nonlinear functions applied inside neural networks so layered models can represent nonlinear relationships.
nodes.importance: Activation functions determine gradient flow, representational capacity, and training behavior in neural networks.

nodes.name: Stochastic Gradient Descent
nodes.description: A gradient-based training method that estimates updates from individual examples or small random subsets of data.
nodes.importance: Stochastic gradient descent makes large-scale learning possible by trading exact gradients for noisy but cheap updates.

nodes.name: Mini-Batch Training
nodes.description: Training that computes updates from small batches of examples rather than the entire dataset or a single example.
nodes.importance: Mini-batches balance noisy gradient estimates, hardware efficiency, and memory constraints in modern neural network training.

nodes.name: Adaptive Optimizers
nodes.description: Gradient-based optimizers such as AdaGrad, RMSProp, and Adam that adapt update sizes from gradient history.
nodes.importance: Adaptive optimizers reduce manual tuning and are widely used for training deep networks and large language models.

references.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

nodes.name: Batch Normalization
nodes.description: A neural network layer that normalizes intermediate activations using batch statistics and learned scale and shift parameters.
nodes.importance: Batch normalization helped stabilize and accelerate deep network training and influenced many later normalization techniques.

nodes.name: Dropout
nodes.description: A regularization technique that randomly omits units during training to reduce co-adaptation and improve generalization.
nodes.importance: Dropout became a standard tool for controlling overfitting in neural networks and inspired broader stochastic regularization methods.

references.title: Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting

nodes.name: Convolutional Neural Networks
nodes.description: Neural networks that use convolutional filters and shared weights to process grid-like data such as images.
nodes.importance: Convolutional neural networks drove major progress in computer vision and remain important for images, video, audio, and scientific signals.

nodes.name: Recurrent Neural Networks
nodes.description: Neural networks with recurrent state connections that process sequences step by step.
nodes.importance: Recurrent neural networks were foundational for sequence modeling before transformers and remain conceptually important for temporal data.

nodes.name: LSTM and GRU
nodes.description: Gated recurrent architectures that improve sequence modeling by controlling how information is stored, updated, and forgotten.
nodes.importance: LSTM and GRU models made recurrent networks more practical for language, speech, time series, and other long-context sequence tasks.

nodes.name: Attention Mechanisms
nodes.description: Neural components that compute context-dependent weighted combinations of information from other positions or representations.
nodes.importance: Attention made sequence models better at long-range dependencies and became the defining operation behind transformer architectures.

references.title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate

nodes.name: Embeddings
nodes.description: Dense vector representations that place discrete items, text, images, users, or entities into learned geometric spaces.
nodes.importance: Embeddings let models compare, retrieve, cluster, and compose symbolic or complex objects using linear algebra.

references.title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space

nodes.name: Sequence-to-Sequence Models
nodes.description: Models that map an input sequence to an output sequence, commonly using encoder-decoder structures.
nodes.importance: Sequence-to-sequence modeling powered major advances in machine translation, summarization, speech, and dialogue systems.

references.title: Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks

nodes.name: Autoencoders
nodes.description: Neural networks trained to reconstruct their inputs through a compressed or structured internal representation.
nodes.importance: Autoencoders connect reconstruction, compression, representation learning, denoising, anomaly detection, and generative modeling.

nodes.name: Generative Adversarial Networks
nodes.description: Generative models trained through a game between a generator that creates samples and a discriminator that distinguishes generated from real data.
nodes.importance: GANs produced influential results in image generation and shaped modern thinking about learned generative objectives.

nodes.name: Diffusion Models
nodes.description: Generative models that learn to reverse a gradual noising process to synthesize data from noise.
nodes.importance: Diffusion models became a leading approach for high-quality image, audio, video, and multimodal generation.

nodes.name: Self-Supervised Learning
nodes.description: Learning representations from unlabeled data by creating predictive training tasks from the data itself.
nodes.importance: Self-supervised learning made it possible to exploit massive unlabeled corpora and underpins many modern foundation models.

nodes.name: Transfer Learning
nodes.description: Reusing knowledge learned from one task, dataset, or model as a starting point for another task.
nodes.importance: Transfer learning reduces data and compute needs and is central to fine-tuning pretrained models.

nodes.name: Foundation Models
nodes.description: Large pretrained models that can be adapted to many downstream tasks through prompting, fine-tuning, or added components.
nodes.importance: Foundation models changed how AI systems are built by making broad pretrained capabilities a reusable platform.

references.title: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models

nodes.name: Natural Language Processing
nodes.description: The field of building computational systems that analyze, generate, retrieve, translate, or interact through human language.
nodes.importance: Natural language processing connects machine learning to search, writing, translation, assistants, coding, education, and knowledge work.

nodes.name: Computer Vision
nodes.description: The field of extracting, recognizing, generating, or acting on information from images, video, and visual sensor data.
nodes.importance: Computer vision powers medical imaging, robotics, autonomous systems, inspection, accessibility, and visual media tools.

references.title: CS231n Convolutional Neural Networks for Visual Recognition

nodes.name: Speech Recognition
nodes.description: The task of converting spoken audio into text or structured linguistic representations.
nodes.importance: Speech recognition enables voice interfaces, transcription, accessibility, call analytics, and multimodal assistants.

nodes.name: Recommender Systems
nodes.description: Systems that predict, rank, or personalize items for users based on behavior, content, context, or learned representations.
nodes.importance: Recommender systems shape media, commerce, education, search, and discovery experiences at large scale.

nodes.name: Information Retrieval
nodes.description: The study of finding relevant documents, passages, items, or facts from large collections in response to an information need.
nodes.importance: Information retrieval underlies search engines, question answering, recommendation, and retrieval-augmented generation systems.

nodes.name: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
nodes.description: A system design that retrieves external information and conditions a generative model on that context before producing an answer.
nodes.importance: Retrieval-augmented generation helps language models use current or domain-specific information while grounding responses in inspectable sources.

references.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

nodes.name: Markov Decision Processes
nodes.description: Mathematical models of sequential decision-making with states, actions, transition probabilities, and rewards.
nodes.importance: Markov decision processes provide the formal foundation for much of reinforcement learning, planning, and control.

nodes.name: Value Functions
nodes.description: Functions that estimate expected long-term return from states, actions, or policies.
nodes.importance: Value functions let reinforcement learning agents compare choices by future consequences rather than immediate reward alone.

nodes.name: Q-Learning
nodes.description: A model-free reinforcement learning algorithm that learns action-value estimates for choosing actions.
nodes.importance: Q-learning is a foundational algorithm for learning control from experience without requiring a known environment model.

nodes.name: ML Pipelines
nodes.description: Repeatable workflows that connect data ingestion, preprocessing, training, evaluation, deployment, and maintenance.
nodes.importance: Pipelines make machine learning systems reproducible, testable, and maintainable across experiments and production releases.

nodes.name: Model Monitoring
nodes.description: Tracking model inputs, outputs, performance, failures, and distribution changes after deployment.
nodes.importance: Monitoring helps teams detect drift, degradation, incidents, and feedback loops that offline evaluation may miss.

references.title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems

nodes.name: Efficient Inference
nodes.description: Techniques such as compression, pruning, quantization, distillation, batching, and hardware-aware serving that reduce inference cost.
nodes.importance: Efficient inference makes deployed models faster, cheaper, lower power, and more practical on edge or high-traffic systems.

nodes.name: Fairness in Machine Learning
nodes.description: The study of how model behavior affects people across groups, contexts, and decision settings, including measurement and mitigation of unfair harms.
nodes.importance: Fairness work helps machine learning systems avoid reinforcing discrimination and supports more accountable deployment decisions.

nodes.name: Model Interpretability
nodes.description: Methods for understanding, explaining, or auditing how models use inputs to produce predictions or decisions.
nodes.importance: Interpretability supports debugging, trust calibration, scientific insight, safety analysis, and regulatory review.

nodes.name: Adversarial Robustness
nodes.description: The study of model behavior under intentionally crafted inputs, distribution shifts, or attacks designed to cause errors.
nodes.importance: Adversarial robustness is important for safety-critical and security-sensitive ML systems where inputs may be malicious or unusual.

nodes.name: Model Cards
nodes.description: Structured reports that document a model's intended use, evaluation results, limitations, ethical considerations, and deployment context.
nodes.importance: Model cards help users, reviewers, and deployers understand model behavior and limits before relying on a system.

nodes.name: Datasheets for Datasets
nodes.description: Structured documentation for datasets covering motivation, collection, composition, recommended uses, distribution, and maintenance.
nodes.importance: Datasheets make dataset assumptions, limitations, and provenance visible, reducing hidden risks in downstream model development.

nodes.name: AI Risk Management
nodes.description: Practices for identifying, measuring, managing, and communicating risks from AI systems across their lifecycle.
nodes.importance: Risk management turns technical evaluation, documentation, monitoring, and governance into a repeatable process for safer deployment.

relationships.note: Statistical estimation and inference rely on probability models.
Derivatives and gradients are central tools for continuous optimization.
Optimization methods often operate on vectors, matrices, and parameter spaces.
Gradient descent is a specific optimization method.
The method uses gradients to decide update directions.
Training examples and model parameters are commonly represented as vectors and matrices.
Prediction, uncertainty, and loss functions often use probabilistic assumptions.
Generalization and evaluation are statistical questions.
Splits and predictions depend on measures of data distribution and impurity.
Decision trees are usually introduced as supervised models.
Many neural networks are trained for supervised prediction tasks.
Layer computations are built from vector and matrix operations.
Training neural networks means optimizing parameters against a loss.
Gradient-based optimizers are the usual way to train neural networks.
Backpropagation is an efficient use of the chain rule.
Backpropagation supplies gradients used by gradient-based training.
The algorithm is defined over layered differentiable models.
Graph theory builds on discrete structures, relations, and proof techniques.
Algorithm analysis often uses discrete structures, counting, logic, and proof.
Efficient algorithms depend on data representations suited to their operations.
Gradient descent is an iterative algorithm with update rules and convergence behavior.
Decision trees use graph-shaped structures with branching paths from root to leaves.
Algorithmic needs shape language features for control flow, abstraction, and data manipulation.
Parallel programs depend on hardware organization, memory systems, and processing units.
GPU computing is a specialized form of massively parallel computation.
GPU acceleration makes large tensor operations and deep learning training practical.
Entropy, coding, and channel models are defined with probabilistic concepts.
Numerical methods approximate derivatives, integrals, and continuous systems.
Many numerical methods operate on matrices, vectors, and linear systems.
Automatic differentiation computes derivatives by applying calculus rules to programs.
Backpropagation can be understood as reverse-mode automatic differentiation through layered models.
Linear regression is commonly expressed with vectors, matrices, and linear systems.
Fitting and evaluating linear regression models uses statistical estimation and uncertainty.
Linear regression is a canonical supervised learning method for numeric prediction.
Bayesian inference updates probability distributions using observed evidence.
Bayesian inference is a statistical framework for estimation and model comparison.
Unsupervised methods use statistical structure, variation, and distributional assumptions.
Clustering, embedding, and dimensionality reduction often depend on vector spaces.
Reinforcement learning models uncertain outcomes, transitions, and expected returns.
Reinforcement learning searches for policies that optimize long-term reward.
Many ensemble methods combine supervised models to improve prediction.
Decision trees are commonly combined into stronger ensemble methods.
Neural networks can learn useful intermediate representations from data.
Deep learning systems rely on scalable gradient training through layered models.
Large language models use gradient-based training through deep neural architectures.
The goal of learning useful representations shaped many deep learning architectures.
Transformers are deep neural architectures built from trainable layers.
Transformer attention and feed-forward blocks rely on vector and matrix operations.
Most modern large language models are built on transformer architectures.
Large language models scale deep learning methods to large text and multimodal corpora.
Language modeling uses ideas related to entropy, prediction, compression, and uncertainty.
Preprocessing starts from collected examples that must be cleaned or transformed.
Train, validation, and test sets are partitions of a dataset.
Datasheets document how datasets were created, maintained, and intended to be used.
Feature engineering modifies or creates input variables.
Feature scaling is a common numeric preprocessing step.
Categorical encoding prepares non-numeric categories for learning algorithms.
Imputation is a preprocessing strategy for incomplete datasets.
Engineered features often improve supervised model performance.
Supervised learning requires target values or labels for training examples.
Supervised models learn from input features paired with targets.
Held-out splits provide evidence about performance on unseen data.
Avoiding leakage shapes how train, validation, and test partitions are built.
Empirical risk is computed from losses over observed examples.
Empirical risk minimization reasons from samples about expected performance.
Training by empirical risk minimization requires optimizing model parameters.
Many supervised training procedures can be framed as empirical risk minimization.
Generalization is the central evaluation question for supervised prediction.
Overfitting is defined by poor generalization relative to training performance.
The bias-variance framework decomposes statistical prediction error.
High variance is one explanation for overfitting behavior.
Regularization is commonly introduced as a way to reduce overfitting.
Regularized linear models constrain coefficients to improve generalization.
Neural networks use regularization to control overfitting and improve robustness.
Cross-validation repeatedly applies the idea of training and testing on held-out splits.
Cross-validation is often used to compare hyperparameter settings.
Classification metrics summarize statistical errors over labeled examples.
Classification metrics evaluate supervised models with categorical targets.
Regression metrics evaluate supervised models with numeric targets.
Calibration compares predicted probabilities with observed frequencies.
A confusion matrix provides raw counts underlying many classification metrics.
Calibration is another way to assess classifiers when predicted probabilities matter.
Logistic regression estimates probabilistic classification models from data.
Logistic regression is commonly expressed with vectors, coefficients, and linear predictors.
Logistic regression is a common classifier evaluated with classification metrics.
Naive Bayes applies probabilistic modeling and Bayes' rule.
Nearest-neighbor prediction depends on examples represented in a feature space.
Feature scaling often matters because k-nearest neighbors uses distances.
Support vector machines are trained through margin-based optimization problems.
Kernel methods rely on inner products and similarity functions in vector spaces.
Kernels allow support vector machines to learn nonlinear decision boundaries.
Gaussian processes define probability distributions over functions.
Gaussian processes use covariance kernels to express function similarity.
Logistic regression is a supervised classification method.
Naive Bayes is commonly used as a supervised classifier.
k-nearest neighbors can perform supervised classification or regression.
Support vector machines are supervised margin-based models.
Gaussian processes are often used for supervised regression and classification.
Random forests are ensembles of decision trees.
Random forests use ensemble averaging to improve prediction stability.
Gradient boosting is a sequential ensemble method.
Gradient boosting improves predictions by optimizing a chosen loss.
Semi-supervised learning extends supervised learning when labeled data is limited.
Semi-supervised learning also uses structure from unlabeled data.
Clustering is a core unsupervised learning task.
k-means is a specific clustering algorithm.
Hierarchical clustering is a family of clustering methods.
DBSCAN is a density-based clustering method.
k-means represents examples and centroids as vectors in a feature space.
Clustering depends on a representation in which similarity can be measured.
Many dimensionality reduction methods operate on matrices and vector spaces.
Dimensionality reduction is a common unsupervised representation task.
PCA is a linear dimensionality reduction method.
PCA is built from covariance matrices, eigenvectors, or singular value decompositions.
Manifold learning is a nonlinear route to lower-dimensional representation.
t-SNE is a popular nonlinear manifold-style visualization method.
Expectation-maximization uses probabilistic latent-variable models.
EM estimates statistical model parameters from incomplete or latent data.
Gaussian mixture models are commonly fit with expectation-maximization.
Gaussian mixture models are probabilistic models of latent cluster membership.
Matrix factorization is defined through matrix products and low-rank approximation.
Matrix factorization is a classic approach for collaborative filtering.
Tensors generalize vectors and matrices to higher dimensions.
Deep learning frameworks represent data, parameters, and activations as tensors.
Computational graphs are structured representations of operations and dependencies.
Automatic differentiation can traverse computational graphs to compute derivatives.
Deep learning systems use computational graphs to organize forward and backward passes.
Activation functions are components inside neural network layers.
Deep networks depend on nonlinear activations for expressive layered computation.
Stochastic gradient descent is a noisy, sample-based variant of gradient descent.
Stochastic gradient methods are central to scalable deep learning training.
Mini-batch training draws small batches from training data.
Mini-batches provide practical gradient estimates for SGD.
Adaptive optimizers build on stochastic gradient updates with learned step-size behavior.
Adaptive optimizers are widely used to train deep neural networks.
Batch normalization is a layer used within neural networks.
Dropout regularizes neural network units during training.
Dropout is a regularization method for neural networks.
Convolutional neural networks are a specialized neural architecture.
CNNs are a major family of deep neural networks.
CNNs are widely used for image and visual recognition tasks.
Recurrent neural networks are neural architectures with recurrent state.
LSTM and GRU models are gated recurrent architectures.
Early sequence-to-sequence models often used recurrent encoder-decoder architectures.
Attention improved encoder-decoder sequence models by focusing on relevant context.
Transformers are built around self-attention mechanisms.
Embeddings represent objects as vectors in learned spaces.
Embeddings are learned representations used across ML systems.
Language models represent tokens and context using embedding spaces.
Autoencoders are neural networks trained to reconstruct inputs.
Autoencoders learn internal representations through reconstruction.
GANs are a deep learning approach to generative modeling.
GANs train neural generator and discriminator models.
Diffusion models use deep networks to learn denoising processes.
Diffusion models are probabilistic generative models over noisy transformations.
Self-supervised learning uses unlabeled data while constructing its own training signal.
Self-supervised objectives are widely used to learn reusable representations.
Many foundation models are pretrained with self-supervised objectives.
Reusable representations make transfer learning effective.
Foundation models are adapted to downstream tasks through transfer learning techniques.
Many modern foundation models use transformer architectures.
Large language models are a prominent class of foundation models.
Many NLP systems are trained on labeled or aligned language tasks.
NLP systems commonly use token, word, sentence, or document embeddings.
Large language models are built for language modeling and NLP tasks.
Modern speech recognition systems rely heavily on deep learning.
Speech recognition connects audio modeling to linguistic output.
Image classification and detection often use supervised training data.
Dense retrieval uses embeddings to search by semantic similarity.
Text retrieval is a central information retrieval task.
RAG systems retrieve relevant information before generation.
RAG commonly conditions large language models on retrieved context.
Many recommender systems learn from explicit or implicit supervised signals.
Embeddings represent users and items for similarity and ranking.
MDPs model uncertain state transitions and expected rewards.
MDPs provide a standard formal model for reinforcement learning problems.
Value functions estimate returns within Markov decision processes.
Q-learning learns action-value functions.
Q-learning is a foundational reinforcement learning algorithm.
Production ML pipelines include repeatable preprocessing steps.
Supervised models often move through pipeline stages from training to evaluation and release.
Pipelines commonly automate validation and model selection procedures.
Monitoring extends pipelines after deployment to track model behavior.
Monitoring can track classification metrics over time when labels become available.
Efficient inference methods are often needed to serve deep models within latency and cost constraints.
Hardware-aware inference uses accelerators and parallel computation efficiently.
Fairness work extends statistical evaluation to harms and performance differences across groups.
Fairness audits often compare error rates and outcomes across groups.
Interpretable model families such as linear regression influenced broader methods for explaining model behavior.
Decision trees are a common example of inherently interpretable models.
Adversarial robustness is a major concern for deep neural models.
Model cards summarize evaluation results, intended uses, and limitations.
Monitoring results can inform ongoing model documentation.
Dataset documentation complements model documentation.
Fairness analysis is one part of managing AI system risk.
Model cards provide documentation that supports lifecycle risk management.
Risk management depends on observing deployed model behavior over time.
